title: Missing Kitty

jokes.text: Narrator introduces Chareth Cutestory as the false identity Michael gave himself
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to have an uncomplicated one-night stand and gives a false identity at his brother's suggestion
jokes.punchline: The name he chose is 'Chareth Cutestory' — an absurdly pompous and ridiculous fake name
jokes.explanation: The name 'Chareth Cutestory' is a brilliant absurdist invention — it sounds like a pretentious maritime-adjacent name (a callback to the yacht club world the Bluths inhabit) while also being utterly ridiculous. It becomes one of the most quoted gags in the episode. The comedy is dense: it says everything about Gob's bad advice and Michael's desperation. High quotability and rewatch value.

jokes.text: He decided to keep seeing her so as not to appear superficial — the woman is blind and can't see his face anyway
jokes.setup: Michael discovers his one-night stand is blind and decides to keep seeing her 'so as not to appear superficial'
jokes.punchline: The irony is total: worrying about appearing superficial to someone who literally cannot see him is the definition of superficiality rationalized away
jokes.explanation: The narrator delivers this with perfect deadpan. The layered irony — he's being superficial about not seeming superficial to someone who cannot perceive his appearance — is quintessential Arrested Development. It's economical, character-true (Michael's self-delusion about being the ethical one), and structurally elegant. Strong 8+ justified.

jokes.text: Maeby's fake illness charity 'B.S.' — 'Not a lot of kids have B.S. these days. What does that stand for? I don't know. It's B.S.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has invented a fake sick persona 'Surely Fünke' to collect giant charity checks, and names the disease B.S.
jokes.punchline: The disease is literally called B.S. and when asked what it stands for, she says 'I don't know. It's B.S.' — both admitting the scam and delivering a perfect double meaning
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of Arrested Development's finest. The wordplay operates on three levels simultaneously: (1) B.S. as an acronym for a fake disease, (2) B.S. as 'bullshit' — the literal admission that it's a scam, (3) Maeby's casual 'I don't know' perfectly characterizing her breezy sociopathy. It's perfectly economical, the punchline is the setup, and only Maeby could deliver it. Earns its 9+.

jokes.text: I figure I'll kill her off just before graduation. Just so everyone gets really sad before prom.
jokes.setup: Maeby is casually explaining her plan for her fake sick alter ego Surely Fünke
jokes.punchline: She has a fully thought-out exit strategy for her fake dying persona, timed specifically for maximum emotional manipulation of her classmates
jokes.explanation: The dark comedy here comes from Maeby treating the fake death of her fake persona as a scheduling problem. The detail of timing it 'just before prom' so everyone is extra sad is wonderfully specific and sociopathic. Strong character integration — this is purely Maeby.

jokes.text: Gob shows up in a garish bright sweater because 'the wife likes me in bright sweaters' — but he only got married days ago on an escalating dare
jokes.setup: Gob, who impulsively married a stranger on a dare, is already doing things to please 'the wife'
jokes.punchline: He's already fully performing husband duties (wearing preferred clothing) for a woman he barely knows and is trying to break up with
jokes.explanation: The comedy comes from Gob being utterly dominated by a woman he married accidentally and is actively trying to leave. The bright sweater is a visual gag made funnier by the context. Strong character integration — Gob's spinelessness with women is a core trait.

jokes.text: Gob's wife uses 'My parents are getting a divorce' to deflect his attempt to break up with her
jokes.setup: Gob tries to break up with his accidental wife
jokes.punchline: Her non-sequitur 'My parents are getting a divorce' derails him completely, suggesting she's weaponizing emotional chaos to keep him
jokes.explanation: A well-timed misdirection — Gob's timing was 'not great.' The wife's non-sequitur deflection perfectly captures the chaotic dysfunction of their relationship. Functional and funny, not exceptional.

jokes.text: Well, least you finally consummated it, huh?
jokes.setup: Gob explains his wife really wants to stay together
jokes.punchline: Michael's response assumes the marriage has been consummated, delivered as a casual silver-lining observation
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical joke. Michael's dry delivery of the assumed silver lining lands well. Not exceptional but well-constructed.

jokes.text: Gob repeats 'Consummated.' with a puzzled/offended tone — reaction beat
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Gob has consummated the marriage
jokes.punchline: Gob's repetition of the word suggests either he doesn't know what it means or he's offended by the implication — either reading is funny
jokes.explanation: Will Arnett's delivery would make this land. The ambiguity — is Gob confused or insulted? — is the joke. Good character work, modest impact.

jokes.text: What is with that 'prosecutie'?
jokes.setup: Gob is asking about Michael's romantic entanglement with the prosecutor
jokes.punchline: He calls her 'prosecutie' — a portmanteau of 'prosecutor' and 'cutie,' perfectly encapsulating Gob's bro-level intelligence
jokes.explanation: A sharp little neologism that's perfectly in Gob's voice. Only Gob would say 'prosecutie.' High character integration and economy. Nearly an 8 — it's the kind of casual wordplay AD excels at.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Okay, Michael. This makes matters better. Let's just destroy it and move on with our lives.'
jokes.setup: Michael says the evidence folder 'makes matters worse'
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately corrects him — it makes matters BETTER — because destruction of evidence is self-evidently good to Gob
jokes.explanation: Good character joke — Gob's criminal pragmatism delivered with complete confidence. The 'move on with our lives' is a nice mundane touch to something deeply criminal.

jokes.text: What is with the insane moral code in this family? Yes, we did it. We're getting rid of the Ten Commandments.
jokes.setup: Gob complains about Michael's 'insane moral code' for not wanting to use a prosecution evidence list
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately chimes in that they've gotten rid of the Ten Commandments (the literal monument outside the courthouse), creating a perfect comic juxtaposition — the family really does have no moral code
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's sharpest jokes. The collision of Gob's complaint about Michael's ethics immediately followed by the family literally removing the Ten Commandments is a perfectly timed ironic juxtaposition. The writing room set this up with impeccable structure. Justifies an 8+.

jokes.text: Honey, they're just heels. They can only support so much weight.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has apparently injured herself at the protest while wearing heels
jokes.punchline: The comment about heels only supporting 'so much weight' is both a practical observation and a fat joke delivered with complete casualness
jokes.explanation: Functional joke, delivered as a throwaway. The deadpan delivery of a semi-mean observation is standard AD fare but not particularly inventive here.

jokes.text: I've always been very passionate about the separation of church and state. [Lindsay, who literally just decided this hours ago]
jokes.setup: Lindsay tells a reporter she's 'always been very passionate about separation of church and state'
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals this conviction began hours ago when she was leaving the courthouse and first saw the monument
jokes.explanation: The gap between Lindsay's self-presentation and the narrator's correction is the core of her character comedy. 'Always been very passionate' vs. 'began hours before' is a perfect encapsulation of Lindsay's performative activism. Strong character integration justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Gob's invented Commandment: 'Thou shalt protect thy father and honor no one above him unless it beith me... thy sweet Lord.'
jokes.setup: Michael accuses Lindsay of conveniently forgetting the Ten Commandments' instruction to honor one's father
jokes.punchline: Gob produces an entirely fabricated Commandment phrased in King James biblical language, seemingly made up on the spot, that perfectly serves his self-interest
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Gob — the confident delivery of absolute nonsense presented as established truth. The invented commandment's specific phrasing ('beith me... thy sweet Lord') is perfectly absurd and the structure of the bit (Michael's legitimate point → Gob's insane fabrication → Michael's deadpan correction) is excellent. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: I'm not sure that one made it down the mountain, Gob.
jokes.setup: Gob quotes his fabricated Commandment
jokes.punchline: Michael's response gently implies it's not real by suggesting it didn't survive the journey from Mount Sinai
jokes.explanation: The perfect button to Gob's fake Commandment. 'Didn't make it down the mountain' is an extraordinarily economical and witty way to call out fabricated scripture without breaking a sweat. This is Michael's comic register at its best — dry, specific, devastating. The economy score of 9.5 is earned.

jokes.text: Buster is injured — 'He was trying to help some handicapped girl up to the bleachers, and he fell. It's just a sprain. But at least it's legitimate.'
jokes.setup: George Michael said 'Someone's going to get hurt' about Maeby's fake disability scam; Buster got hurt trying to help a 'handicapped' girl (probably Surely/Maeby)
jokes.punchline: It's a legitimate injury — ironic contrast to Maeby's fake B.S. condition. 'At least it's legitimate' lands as a punchline on the whole B.S. subplot
jokes.explanation: The callback to George Michael's prediction paying off through Buster's legitimate injury — the contrast of 'legitimate' against the B.S. disease is the punchline. Good structural comedy, pays off an earlier setup.

jokes.text: Ah, le clumsy adolescence. It's a phase we've all been through. Except for me. I was like a cat. I always ended up on all fours.
jokes.setup: Buster has sprained his ankle being clumsy
jokes.punchline: Tobias's self-exception from normal human development ('I was like a cat') and 'always ended up on all fours' — which has an obvious double meaning and foreshadows his catlike agility subplot later in the episode
jokes.explanation: This is excellent AD layering. 'Always ended up on all fours' is an inadvertent Tobias double entendre (his obliviousness to his implied homosexuality), AND it plants the 'cat' motif that will pay off when he literally tries to use 'catlike agility' to break into Maggie's house. Rewatch bonus is high — on first viewing it's funny; on rewatch you see the foreshadowing. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Tobias mimes/demonstrates 'like a cat' — reaction beat as others stare
jokes.setup: Tobias has described himself as catlike
jokes.punchline: He physically demonstrates it — crouching/posing 'like a cat' — to the visible bafflement of the family
jokes.explanation: The physical demonstration of the cat claim extends the joke well. David Cross committing fully to the bit is what makes it land. Rewatch value because this cat motif returns repeatedly.

jokes.text: No one's gonna sue anybody. I'm not gonna have my son start abusing the legal system like the rest of this family. After all, we're gonna need it for years to come.
jokes.setup: Gob suggests George Michael sue the school over the lack of bleacher ramps
jokes.punchline: Michael nobly refuses to abuse the legal system — then undercuts his own righteousness by admitting they'll need it for years (i.e., for Dad's trial and ongoing Bluth crimes)
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-aware self-contradiction — protecting the legal system while acknowledging he's going to exploit it for his criminal family — is solid character irony. Well-constructed and in his voice.

jokes.text: Well, I'm not the main idiot, but I'm the idiot in charge.
jokes.setup: Michael is confessing to Maggie that he's a Bluth, saying 'I might even be the main idiot'
jokes.punchline: He immediately self-corrects to 'not the main idiot, but the idiot in charge' — a distinction without a difference that's delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is sharp Michael Bluth comedy — his perpetual need to position himself as the responsible one even while confessing total irresponsibility. The distinction between 'main idiot' and 'idiot in charge' is a perfect absurdist self-assessment delivered deadpan. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: You feel sincere. / Damn it. I have spent the last five hours hating you...
jokes.setup: Michael apologizes and says 'if you could see my face you'd see how sorry I am'
jokes.punchline: Maggie says 'you feel sincere' (she's 'blind') then immediately reveals she's been hating him for five hours — having already known who he was in court
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Maggie already knew layers the scene with retroactive comedy. 'You feel sincere' as her reading of the situation — since she's pretending to be blind — is a nice double deception moment.

jokes.text: Maggie: 'You feel sincere.' — after Michael says she'd see how sorry he is if she could see his face
jokes.setup: Michael apologizes and says 'if you could see my face, you'd see how sorry I am' — addressing what he thinks is her blindness
jokes.punchline: Maggie responds 'You feel sincere' — touching his face, which is a sincerely blind-person move, but laden with irony since she can see perfectly
jokes.explanation: Works on multiple levels on rewatch — Maggie is performing blindness and her 'sincere' assessment is itself ironic since she is being deeply deceptive. Short, sharp, economical. Rewatch bonus for the dramatic irony layer.

jokes.text: Maggie reveals she already knew Michael was Michael Bluth — 'I'm blind, Michael. I'm not stuffed up. You smelled me.'
jokes.setup: Michael is shocked that Maggie knew his real identity despite his disguise
jokes.punchline: She identified him by smell — a real blind-person skill deployed here for comedic effect, made funnier by her clarification that being blind doesn't mean being 'stuffed up'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection — the audience expects the reveal to be about sight (maybe Maggie isn't blind), but instead it doubles down on her blindness through smell. 'I'm not stuffed up' is a great specific detail. Rewatch bonus because we later learn she CAN see, making her performance here even more layered.

jokes.text: I'm blind, Michael. / I'm not stuffed up. / You smelled me. / I smelled you, baby.
jokes.setup: Michael asks how Maggie knew who he was in court — he assumes she heard his voice
jokes.punchline: She smelled him. 'I smelled you, baby.' The delivery of 'baby' after the clinical 'I smelled you' is the button
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed misdirection — Michael assumes voice recognition, she corrects to smell, and then the seductive 'baby' undercuts the absurdity. The exchange is snappy and builds perfectly.

jokes.text: I was manipulating you. This isn't the real list. This is a wish list.
jokes.setup: Maggie gave Michael a folder she claimed was a summary of evidence against his father
jokes.punchline: It was a wish list — she was playing him the whole time
jokes.explanation: The plot reversal is funny — the person supposedly being manipulated (Maggie) was manipulating right back. 'This is a wish list' is a clean punchline. Good structural joke.

jokes.text: Well, you lied to me. Chareth Cutestory? / I know. 'Thou shalt not make up a name in vain.'
jokes.setup: Michael is called out for his fake name
jokes.punchline: Michael responds with a Commandment parody — 'Thou shalt not make up a name in vain' — a callback to the Ten Commandments subplot and a pun on 'take the Lord's name in vain'
jokes.explanation: This is a densely layered callback that ties the episode's two main subplots together. 'Thou shalt not make up a name in vain' works as (1) a parody commandment like Gob's earlier fake one, (2) a pun on the actual commandment, (3) a self-aware confession. It's economical, witty, and rewards attention. Strong 8+ material.

jokes.text: Look at me. This could get me... Are you actually looking at me? / Yes, I am.
jokes.setup: Maggie says 'look at me' rhetorically while making a point — Michael checks if she's literally looking at him
jokes.punchline: She says 'yes, I am' — confirming she can see, while maintaining her blind act with him (dramatic irony for the audience who doesn't know yet, or a slip she recovers from)
jokes.explanation: This is a rewatch joke — on first viewing it reads as a small funny moment, on rewatch you realize she IS looking at him because she can see. The dramatic irony makes it retroactively better. Solid AD layering.

jokes.text: It's like we're making naughty sex even naughtier.
jokes.setup: Michael and Maggie have slept together again despite it being wildly unethical — she's prosecuting his father
jokes.punchline: Maggie's framing of their ethical violation as simply 'naughty sex made naughtier' is absurdly reductive and characterizes her perfectly
jokes.explanation: A fun line that captures the absurdity of their situation by reducing it to titillation. Works well in context.

jokes.text: This is the second most unethical thing I've ever done.
jokes.setup: Michael is agonizing about sleeping with the prosecutor of his father's case
jokes.punchline: He ranks it — it's only the SECOND most unethical thing, implying he's done something even worse and he's keeping score
jokes.explanation: Michael's persistent self-positioning as the ethical one is undercut brilliantly — he keeps a hierarchy of his own ethical violations. The 'second most' implies a ranked list that he has apparently thought about. Classic Michael irony. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Well, it's like the Ten Commandments saying. 'Be true to thine own self, and to thine own self... Be true.' Number seven.
jokes.setup: Michael and Maggie are trying to justify their unethical affair with platitudes
jokes.punchline: The 'Ten Commandments saying' they invoke is actually a misquote of Shakespeare (Hamlet), attributed to the wrong source, and numbered incorrectly — which is the seventh commandment (adultery, ironically)
jokes.explanation: Densely layered: they misattribute Shakespeare to the Ten Commandments while rationalizing adultery, and the commandment they cite ('number seven') is literally 'Thou shalt not commit adultery.' It's a callback to the episode's Commandments subplot and works on multiple levels. Rewatch bonus for the irony of the numbering. Justifies 8.

jokes.text: I think I'm ready now. / All right. / Okay.
jokes.setup: Michael says 'I think I'm ready now' after their conversation about keeping the affair secret
jokes.punchline: The beat of 'All right / Okay' implies they are about to have sex again immediately after resolving to keep it secret — the scene cuts away
jokes.explanation: A well-timed implication — their whole conversation about ethics ends with immediate surrender. The economy of 'I think I'm ready now / All right / Okay' doing all that work is good sitcom writing.

jokes.text: Flashback: Gob switching the evidence folder while his siblings argued about the Ten Commandments
jokes.setup: We learn Gob had the real evidence folder all along; he switched it out using sleight of hand during the family argument
jokes.punchline: The flashback reveals the evidence swap happened during the exact distraction of the Ten Commandments argument we already watched — retroactive comedy of the family's own chaos enabling Gob's scheme
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff is excellent — the earlier scene we watched was simultaneously Gob executing a sleight-of-hand magic trick. The narrator's 'they don't call me a sleight-of-hand artist for nothing' is the reveal. High rewatch value. Good AD structural comedy.

jokes.text: Gob reveals he secretly switched the evidence file: 'They don't call me a sleight-of-hand artist for nothing.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. receives the real evidence file — raising the question of how he got it
jokes.punchline: Gob, a professional illusionist/magician, used his one actual skill to pull off a legitimate caper — the joke is that his magic is actually useful for once
jokes.explanation: Good character-based plot reveal. Gob's magician skills being the actual mechanism of a real crime is a satisfying joke for fans who know his character.

jokes.text: Flashback juxtaposition: The SAME scene of Lindsay saying 'I've always been very passionate about the separation of church and state' plays again, immediately after showing Gob's sleight-of-hand
jokes.setup: The show replays the earlier scene verbatim as context for the flashback
jokes.punchline: The repetition of Lindsay's claim in a new context makes it even funnier — she was obliviously providing cover for Gob's criminal maneuvering
jokes.explanation: The structural joke here is the show's trademark 'Rashomon' editing — the same footage plays but means something entirely different in context. Lindsay's passionate advocacy was unknowingly giving Gob cover to commit a crime. This is craft-level comedy writing.

jokes.text: Barry hasn't read the evidence file because 'I had a really interesting date last night. A woman who actually works two jobs.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille are expecting Barry to have analyzed the evidence; he produces it and they assume he's read it
jokes.punchline: Barry confesses he hasn't read it because he was distracted by a date, then justifies it by noting she had 'two jobs' — a detail that reveals his own low bar for being impressed
jokes.explanation: Barry Zuckerkorn's complete incompetence as a lawyer is a running gag. The specificity of 'a woman who actually works two jobs' as the thing that impressed him (implying most of his dates don't work) is funny character-specific detail. Good Barry moment.

jokes.text: Uh, you. I was raising the children. When was the last time you raised children? Excuse me? I've got two in the waiting room right this moment.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille argue about whose fault the criminal evidence against them is
jokes.punchline: Lucille claims she was 'raising the children' as her alibi; George Sr. fires back that she has 'two in the waiting room right now' — i.e., Buster and Annyong, fully grown adults she still treats as young children
jokes.explanation: The punchline recontextualizes 'raising children' to expose Lucille's infantilization of Buster (and her absurd adoption of Annyong). Strong character integration for Lucille.

jokes.text: They don't tell you anything, huh? / They tell me things. [beat] / It doesn't matter though, because Dad is gonna be in here a long time. / No. We have court day today. They take plea. Daddy come home. You out.
jokes.setup: Buster and Annyong are waiting outside while the adults meet; Buster feels excluded
jokes.punchline: Annyong (who usually says only his name) reveals he has more information than Buster AND delivers it as a threat — 'Daddy come home. You out.' — threatening Buster's position in the household
jokes.explanation: Annyong breaking from his one-word persona to deliver a perfectly timed threat is a great character subversion. 'Daddy come home. You out.' is delivered as a genuine power move that humiliates Buster. The inversion of who's really in charge is sharp. Strong 8.

jokes.text: Buster's flashback to George Sr.'s pre-arrest threat: 'Hey, enjoy yourself tonight, because you are out of here. I'm not gonna spend my retirement watching you wipe your nose on your sleeve.'
jokes.setup: Annyong's threat triggers Buster's memory of when George Sr. made the same threat before his arrest
jokes.punchline: The specific detail of 'I'm not gonna spend my retirement watching you wipe your nose on your sleeve' — this is how George Sr. describes his grown son's behavior
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the nose-wiping detail is what makes this land. Buster's childishness is encapsulated in one image that George Sr. has clearly been storing as a grievance. Good character work.

jokes.text: I can't breathe, Dad. / Neither can I.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is tying Buster's tie and pulling it too tight in the prison visit flashback
jokes.punchline: Buster says he can't breathe; George Sr. deadpans 'Neither can I' — suggesting the emotional suffocation of parenting Buster, or possibly the physical confinement of prison
jokes.explanation: A compact, dark two-liner. The economy is high — 'Neither can I' is doing double emotional duty. Slight but effective.

jokes.text: I think I'm gonna say that I signed myself out about 10 minutes ago. / You're right. You can't afford to get caught. I don't need my lawyer in prison. We'll have Gob break in.
jokes.setup: Barry, who is present at the prison illegally, announces his plan to claim he signed out
jokes.punchline: George Sr. agrees — he can't have his lawyer in prison, so they'll send Gob instead. As if Gob is the expendable one, but also as if Gob is capable of a real crime
jokes.explanation: Barry's casual criminal incompetence and George Sr.'s immediate pivot to 'send Gob' reveals how all the Bluths view each other. Decent character comedy.

jokes.text: Well, gee, Dad, I've got a wife now. I don't think that I can afford to get caught either. / Good point. You might want to change out of that sweater.
jokes.setup: George Sr. asks Gob to break into the prosecutor's house; Gob protests he has a wife now
jokes.punchline: George Sr. acknowledges the marriage is a liability — then immediately pivots to the practical concern of the bright sweater being too visible for a break-in
jokes.explanation: A good callback to the sweater gag that recontextualizes it — the wife-pleasing sweater is now a criminal liability. George Sr.'s practical concern about the sweater while ignoring the moral concern of asking his son to commit burglary is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Hey, there's the cat. Sorry you couldn't have been down at the prison with us.
jokes.setup: Gob encounters Tobias and calls him 'the cat' — referencing Tobias's earlier claim to have catlike agility
jokes.punchline: Gob has adopted 'the cat' as Tobias's nickname, played with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A light callback that keeps the cat motif alive. The nickname 'the cat' coming from Gob is amusing given his own delusions. Sets up the later cat agility subplot.

jokes.text: He feels you've been sucking off this family far too long. If you don't start pulling your weight around here, it's gonna be shape up or ship up.
jokes.setup: Gob relays George Sr.'s criticism of Tobias to his face
jokes.punchline: Gob mangles 'shape up or ship out' as 'shape up or ship UP' — a malapropism — while the phrase 'sucking off this family' is an inadvertent double entendre in the Tobias context
jokes.explanation: The mangled idiom ('ship up') is in Gob's character, and 'sucking off this family' fits the Tobias double-entendre tradition. Competent AD gag.

jokes.text: This guy's the real deal. He's on the inside. / But, he's got some demands. / This could be the break we wanted.
jokes.setup: Maggie is excited about an inside witness with demands
jokes.punchline: The 'inside man' turns out to be Buster — who then proceeds to ask for immunity for himself and his mom and to have Annyong sent back to Korea — the most irrelevant and petty list of demands imaginable
jokes.explanation: The buildup of 'the real deal' and 'demands' followed by Buster's pathetic wish list (immunity for mom, Annyong sent to Korea) is a well-structured misdirection. Buster's petty score-settling with Annyong at his most 'serious' moment is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: I want immunity for me and my mom... and I want my dad to stay in prison. Also, can you send Annyong back to Korea?
jokes.setup: Buster is supposedly being a serious informant with demands
jokes.punchline: His demands escalate from reasonable (immunity) to bizarre (keep dad in prison) to petty and absurd (send Annyong back to Korea)
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The escalating demands work perfectly: (1) immunity — sensible, (2) keep dad in prison — darker betrayal, (3) send Annyong back to Korea — suddenly petty and hilarious. The third demand reveals that Buster's entire motivation for betraying his family may be sibling rivalry with a 10-year-old. Only Buster. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Meanwhile, Maggie's interrogation of Buster was proving fruitless.
jokes.setup: Buster has just offered to be a witness with demands
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately describes the interrogation as 'proving fruitless' — despite Buster thinking he's been enormously helpful
jokes.explanation: The narrator's tone is the joke. His deadpan assessment of Buster's uselessness as a witness, despite Buster feeling heroic, is a clean piece of AD narrator comedy.

jokes.text: He came back home once with a black statue with an erect penis. It stayed in our living room until my aunt objected.
jokes.setup: Maggie asks Buster if his father came back from overseas trips with anything unusual
jokes.punchline: Buster's answer — a phallic statue that lived in the living room for some time before an aunt objected — is delivered with complete guileless sincerity
jokes.explanation: The specificity ('erect penis,' 'until my aunt objected') makes this land. Buster treating this as normal family history is perfectly in character. The suggestion that the family had a phallic statue in the living room as a conversation piece fits the Bluth world.

jokes.text: This guy's wasting our time. Take away his grilled cheese.
jokes.setup: Buster is failing to provide useful information in interrogation
jokes.punchline: Maggie's punishment is to remove his grilled cheese — confirming that the only reason Buster cooperated was for the food
jokes.explanation: The grilled cheese as Buster's weakness is a beautiful small detail — it tells you everything about his infantile character and why he agreed to testify. Maggie's matter-of-fact removal of it as interrogation leverage is darkly funny. 'Take away his grilled cheese' is highly quotable.

jokes.text: Buster tells Maggie the evidence is at a house on Scenic View Drive. / Isn't that where you live? / Yeah. And I'm headed there now.
jokes.setup: Buster offers the location of the evidence boxes as valuable testimony
jokes.punchline: It's Maggie's own house — she obviously already knows where she lives — and Buster is obliviously proud of himself
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully executed dramatic irony joke. Buster's revelation — delivered with the self-satisfaction of a great informant — is information the prosecutor already possesses about her own home. 'And I'm headed there now' is the perfect button. High impact, well-structured. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Tobias uses his catlike agility to get the evidence. 'The cat is in.'
jokes.setup: Tobias was tasked with breaking into Maggie's house using his claimed catlike agility
jokes.punchline: 'The cat is in' — Tobias has adopted the cat persona fully and narrates his own mission in cat terms
jokes.explanation: Tobias playing spy in his self-mythologized cat identity is good character comedy. The callback chain is well-maintained. 'The cat is in' is succinct and funny.

jokes.text: Tobias concerned about Maggie's heightened sense of smell, sets off to cloak himself in her scent — spends extended time in her bathrobe/perfume area while she walks around the house
jokes.setup: Tobias breaks into Maggie's house just as she returns; he's trapped and worried about her heightened blind-person smell detection
jokes.punchline: His solution is to 'cloak himself in her scent' — by dousing himself in her perfume/bathrobe, which is simultaneously desperate, absurd, and intensely creepy
jokes.explanation: The entire sequence — Tobias sneaking around a house while a supposedly blind woman navigates it — is extended physical comedy. The 'cloaking scent' logic is absurdist but internally consistent with cat behavior (cats mark territory). It's also deeply creepy in a way that characterizes Tobias's obliviousness. Strong visual comedy sequence.

jokes.text: Maggie comes home unexpectedly — Tobias must hide from a blind woman, except she can see him
jokes.setup: Tobias has broken in and Maggie arrives home; he believes she is blind so he only needs to avoid sound
jokes.punchline: Maggie can actually see him perfectly but cannot reveal she can see without betraying her blindness act — creating a prolonged game of cat-and-mouse where both parties are operating under false pretenses
jokes.explanation: This is the comedic centerpiece of the episode and a masterclass in situational comedy. The premise is airtight: a woman pretending to be blind must pretend NOT to see an intruder, while the intruder believes he only needs to be quiet. The double-deception creates an escalating comedy of errors that is perfectly constructed. The structure earns 9 — every element of both characters' lies creates the exact situation that traps them both. Earns 8.5+ comfortably.

jokes.text: Justice is blind. / Justice cannot be blind, Doc. He's got a handle and everything. Come on, boy.
jokes.setup: Michael brings Maggie's guide dog 'Justice' to the vet, who tells him 'Justice is blind'
jokes.punchline: Michael takes it literally ('He's got a handle and everything') while the vet means the dog is actually blind — the phrase is also the legal/philosophical maxim, adding a third layer
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a classic AD wordplay construction. 'Justice is blind' operates on three levels simultaneously: (1) the dog's name is Justice and it is literally blind, (2) the legal maxim 'justice is blind' describes the court system that is prosecuting his father, (3) Michael's literalistic rejection ('he's got a handle and everything') is perfectly in character. The naming of the dog 'Justice' was set up earlier and pays off here brilliantly. Rewatch bonus for appreciating the full architecture. Justifies 8.8+.

jokes.text: Maybe his peripheral isn't great... but he's been leading a blind woman around. / I think she's been leading him around. / But that would mean that Maggie isn't blind.
jokes.setup: Michael defends the blind dog's ability to guide Maggie
jokes.punchline: The vet's correction triggers Michael's deductive revelation — if the blind dog isn't leading her, she must not be blind. The logic chain is played perfectly straight while being absurd
jokes.explanation: The major plot reveal executed as comedy — a detective logic chain that leads to the truth through absurdist deduction. 'Maybe his peripheral isn't great' is a hilarious piece of motivated reasoning from Michael. The structure of setup → defense → correction → realization is textbook comedy writing. Strong 8+.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Justice cannot be blind, Doc. He's got a handle and everything.'
jokes.setup: Vet confirms the seeing-eye dog is blind
jokes.punchline: Michael's logical counter-argument — the dog has a harness handle, therefore cannot be blind — is both an example of Michael's lawyer-brain and complete denial
jokes.explanation: This is the follow-up beat to the 'Justice is blind' pun — Michael doubling down. 'He's got a handle and everything' is the kind of absurdly specific rationalization that's perfectly Michael. Scored separately as it's a distinct joke beat.

jokes.text: In fact, Maggie wasn't blind... but had been pretending to be since she found it helpful in taking the LSATs.
jokes.setup: The reveal that Maggie has been faking blindness
jokes.punchline: The origin of the fake blindness is that she used it to cheat on the LSATs — an incredibly petty and absurdly escalated life decision that she then maintained as a long-term identity
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest moments. The origin of Maggie's blindness scam — LSATs — is spectacularly petty and escalatory. She cheated on law school entrance exams and liked the advantages so much she maintained it for her entire career as a PROSECUTOR. The irony is complete: a lying prosecutor who literally can't see (the law). The deadpan narrator delivery makes it land harder. The parallel with Maeby's B.S. scam (fake disability for personal gain) is an elegant episode-level structural mirror. Justifies 8.8+.

jokes.text: Don't worry. You're so ahead. [Maggie to a witness in court]
jokes.setup: Maggie is shown in court seemingly functioning as a blind person
jokes.punchline: She tells a witness 'you're so ahead' — which is a sighted person's casual assessment of visual advantage that no blind person would say this way. It's a slip that retroactively signals her deception
jokes.explanation: In context of the narrator's reveal, this is a retroactive rewatch joke — you see Maggie letting her sighted nature slip. Modest on first viewing, stronger on rewatch.

jokes.text: Her ability to see, however, was currently proving a disadvantage... as she couldn't reveal to the man she recognized as Tobias that she knew he was there without betraying her secret.
jokes.setup: Tobias is sneaking around Maggie's house while she navigates it as if blind
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the irony: Maggie can see Tobias but can't acknowledge it without revealing she's not blind — so both are trapped in competing deceptions
jokes.explanation: The structural comedy here is exceptional — two people trapped in competing deceptions, each unable to act normally. This is the kind of farce architecture that AD excels at. The narrator articulating the exact nature of the trap is very funny because it highlights the absurd specificity of the situation. The extended cat-and-mouse sequence that follows earns its laughs through pure commitment to the premise. Justifies 8.7.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Tobias was finding her an uncannily difficult blind girl to outmaneuver.' — as Maggie tracks his every move while pretending not to see
jokes.setup: The prolonged cat-and-mouse farce between Tobias (hiding from a supposed blind woman) and Maggie (pretending not to see)
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan observation that Tobias finds a 'blind girl' difficult to outmaneuver — because she can actually see him perfectly
jokes.explanation: The narrator's understatement ('uncannily difficult') is beautifully calibrated — it treats Maggie's omniscience as a puzzling anomaly rather than revealing the truth. This is the narrator comedy at its best: telling the truth while concealing it. Strong economy and structure.

jokes.text: Their game of cat and mouse continued. [Extended visual sequence of Tobias sneaking while Maggie 'navigates blind']
jokes.setup: Tobias tries to hide from Maggie who can see him but pretends she can't
jokes.punchline: The extended silent sequence of both pretending — Tobias going to elaborate hiding lengths while Maggie acts blind despite watching him — is pure visual farce
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'game of cat and mouse' label is itself a callback to the cat motif while accurately describing the farce structure. The physical comedy of this sequence is strong visual television comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, Justice, what is it, boy? What is it? Is there some jackass in my bathrobe?
jokes.setup: Michael arrives to return Justice; the dog presumably signals toward Tobias hiding in Maggie's bathrobe
jokes.punchline: Maggie's question 'Is there some jackass in my bathrobe?' — asking the dog — is the punchline, both funny as a question to a dog and devastatingly accurate (Tobias, who cloaked himself in her bathrobe's scent, IS in her bathrobe)
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable and excellently structured. Asking a blind guide dog 'is there some jackass in my bathrobe' is funny on its face. That Tobias literally IS in (or wearing/having wrapped himself in) her bathrobe as a scent-cloaking measure makes it a perfect payoff to the earlier setup. Economy of language is exceptional. Strong 8+.

jokes.text: Oh! I can't see! [Tobias, revealed from the bathrobe]
jokes.setup: Tobias is caught and exposed
jokes.punchline: Tobias shouts 'I can't see!' — either because he's in the bathrobe/blinded by it, or as a panicked attempt to maintain his own cover. The echo of Maggie's fake blindness claim is the deeper joke
jokes.explanation: The echo of Maggie's blindness claim coming from Tobias is a nice structural mirror. His panic or genuine bathrobe-blindness lands as physical comedy. Rewatch bonus for the parallel.

jokes.text: They don't have a case against us. I took care of everything. / Something smells wonderful. / Well, thank you. Would that I could say that I wore it to impress you, but no, I'm afraid it's merely a cloaking agent from a sadly blunderous afternoon.
jokes.setup: The family gathers at the courthouse; Gob is confident they've won; Lucille compliments something smelling wonderful
jokes.punchline: Tobias clarifies the smell is his 'cloaking agent' — Maggie's perfume he doused himself in — described as coming from a 'sadly blunderous afternoon.' The formal, theatrical way he describes his catastrophic failure is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Sadly blunderous afternoon' is vintage Tobias — theatrical self-description of failure using language no one else would choose. The word 'blunderous' is a Tobias malapropism/invention. High character integration and quotability. Justifies 8.3.

jokes.text: Gob re: Buster as the family's potential informant: 'Worst-case scenario he gets caught, no more Buster.'
jokes.setup: The family is discussing who they have available to help their legal situation
jokes.punchline: Gob frames Buster's potential arrest as a best-possible outcome, not a worst-case — 'worst-case scenario, no more Buster' delivered as if losing Buster would be no great loss
jokes.explanation: Dark and economical. The casual dismissal of Buster's wellbeing as a silver lining is perfectly in character for Gob. 'No more Buster' as a 'worst-case' is a nice inversion.

jokes.text: I'm not going anywhere. You're the one who's staying put. Not me. You. / What's gotten into you? Have you been eating cheese?
jokes.setup: Buster is standing up to Lucille after his 'empowering' interrogation experience
jokes.punchline: Lucille's response to Buster's uncharacteristic defiance is 'Have you been eating cheese?' — as if his only possible explanation for standing up to her is a dietary change
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Lucille's 'have you been eating cheese?' as an explanation for Buster's assertiveness is perfect Bluth family comedy — it infantilizes Buster completely while being absurdly specific. It implies that (1) Buster doesn't usually make decisions, (2) cheese somehow gives him energy, (3) Lucille has clearly made this connection before. Only Lucille. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.4+.

jokes.text: Surely Fünke address the crowd: 'Not just to me, but to all handicapped kids out there who are sick and tired of sitting in the front rows at basketball games.'
jokes.setup: Maeby as 'Surely Fünke' is giving an impassioned speech to the protest crowd
jokes.punchline: Her complaint on behalf of all handicapped kids is that they have to sit in the FRONT ROWS at basketball games — the complaint is that their special accommodation is actually a great seat, which she's framing as a hardship
jokes.explanation: An excellent joke — Maeby's activism complaint on behalf of 'handicapped kids' is that they get front-row seats at games, which is clearly not a complaint any actual handicapped person would make. It exposes the fraudulence of her advocacy while being a funny observation in its own right. The irony is dense: she's fighting for the rights of a population she's impersonating, about a benefit she'd actually want. Strong 8.3.

jokes.text: George Michael spotted his father and, not wanting to appear to be abusing the legal system, found a place to hide. / Michael, determined to expose Maggie, hid to avoid tipping his hand.
jokes.setup: Both Michael and George Michael are at the courthouse for separate reasons
jokes.punchline: Both independently decide to hide from each other — father hiding from son, son hiding from father — each for their own reasons, unaware of the other's motive. The simultaneous hiding is a visual gag the narrator describes
jokes.explanation: The parallel hiding is a well-structured comic beat — the sitcom coincidence executed with AD's characteristic deadpan narration making explicit what we're seeing. Good structural comedy.

jokes.text: Michael also hides — 'Michael, determined to expose Maggie, hid to avoid tipping his hand.' Both Michael AND George Michael are hiding from each other at the courthouse.
jokes.setup: Both father and son are hiding at the courthouse for different reasons
jokes.punchline: The visual absurdity of both protagonists hiding from each other — Michael to catch Maggie, George Michael to avoid Michael — is a perfect situational comedy convergence
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed farce moment — the parallel hiding, neither aware of the other, creates the kind of structural comedy this show excels at. The narrator's dry observation that Michael is hiding to expose a liar (while being a liar himself) adds irony.

jokes.text: And for what it's worth, George Michael, you cannot let people get away with that. / It's just so hard to know what the right thing to do is. / I know it. It's not like there's some list of rules handed down to us from on high. / Watch your head.
jokes.setup: Michael is advising George Michael not to let people get away with lying to him, saying 'it's not like there's some list of rules handed down from on high'
jokes.punchline: At exactly that moment the Ten Commandments monument (being brought down by Lindsay's protest) nearly hits them — 'Watch your head' is the narrated physical punchline
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and among the finest in the entire series. The construction is impeccable: Michael tells his son 'it's not like there's some list of rules handed down to us from on high' — at the precise moment the literal Ten Commandments monument (a stone tablet, handed down by God, from on high) nearly falls on them, requiring the physical 'watch your head.' Every word is doing work: 'handed down from on high' describes both the narrative origin of the Commandments and their literal descent from the courthouse. The irony is multiple — Michael is lecturing about ethics while conducting an affair with a prosecutor, and the Ten Commandments are the thing he just watched his family remove. It's a perfect episode-length payoff. Justifies 9.2.

jokes.text: 'It's not like there's some list of rules handed down to us from on high.' Ten Commandments fall on Michael's head.
jokes.setup: Michael says there's no 'list of rules from on high' — directly calling back the Ten Commandments plot
jokes.punchline: Immediately, the Ten Commandments (the literal granite ones) fall and hit him — 'Watch your head' — making Michael literally struck by the list of rules from on high he just dismissed
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and earns a 9+. The setup is Michael's hypocritical speech to his son about rules and lying. The punchline is the entire Ten Commandments plot resolving into a physical punchline that is simultaneously: (1) slapstick, (2) divine punishment/irony, (3) a callback to the entire subplot, (4) foreshadowed by Gob's joke Commandments, (5) a visual pun on 'handed down from on high,' and (6) perfectly timed. The 'Watch your head' from a passerby who has clearly seen this before is a stunning economy. This is the kind of multi-layered comedic payoff that defines the show. Comparable to the calibration anchor examples in quality.

jokes.text: Your Honor, the purpose of a court of law is to find the truth... after all, they say justice is blind. Surely, she has enough sight to catch the truth before it hits her in the face.
jokes.setup: Michael delivers a courtroom speech designed to expose Maggie as a fake blind person
jokes.punchline: He throws a Bible at her face — 'catch the truth before it hits her in the face' — if she's blind, she can't catch it; if she catches it, she's revealed
jokes.explanation: Michael's Bible throw is a clever and funny plan — using the 'justice is blind' phrase as setup for a physical test. The layering of 'justice is blind' (the dog's name, the legal maxim, and now Maggie's fake condition) pays off here. The courtroom plan is absurd but internally logical.

jokes.text: Unfortunately, Maggie had been temporarily blinded that day. / I can't see! / Bailiff. / That was my plan. What do you got?
jokes.setup: Michael throws the Bible at Maggie to expose her fake blindness; she responds with 'I can't see!'
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals she WAS actually temporarily blinded by Tobias's earlier spraying (pepper spray? perfume in eyes?) — so her reaction is genuine. Michael's plan backfired perfectly. 'That was my plan. What do you got?' is his desperate pivot
jokes.explanation: The triple-layered misdirection is executed brilliantly: Michael tries to reveal a fake blind person → she was genuinely blinded by Tobias's earlier chaos → Michael's expose becomes a failure AND he accidentally proves her blindness. His 'That was my plan. What do you got?' is the perfect comedian's pivot-when-caught. Very quotable and high impact. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: And he will carry on my legacy and put an end to B.S. / Surely can beat this thing. / No more B.S. No more B.S.
jokes.setup: Maeby/Surely has made George Michael the public face of her anti-B.S. campaign
jokes.punchline: The crowd chants 'No more B.S.!' as a genuine rallying cry — while George Michael, who knows it's literally bullshit, is forced to be the spokesperson. The chant is simultaneously sincere and a meta-admission of the scam
jokes.explanation: The B.S. payoff is satisfying — a crowd sincerely chanting 'No more B.S.' in support of an actual B.S. scam. George Michael's trapped compliance is great cringe comedy. Good callback payoff to the episode's running B.S. gag.

jokes.text: Crowd chants 'No more B.S.! No more B.S.!' — unknowingly chanting for the end of bullshit
jokes.setup: The crowd supports Surely's cause and chants her slogan
jokes.punchline: Their sincere chant for the fake disease's end is literally a demand for an end to bullshit — the audience is earnestly demanding honesty while being deceived
jokes.explanation: The full payoff of the B.S. wordplay — a crowd earnestly demanding 'no more B.S.' while being fed B.S. is a beautifully ironic image. The double meaning fires perfectly at this scale. Strong structure.

jokes.text: I think I'm gonna go for 'I was struck by the Bible and it restored my sight.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Maggie if she'll admit to faking blindness now that she's been kicked off the case
jokes.punchline: Her cover story — struck by the Bible, sight restored — is an absurdly convenient miracle narrative that also directly references Michael's earlier Bible-throw attempt to expose her
jokes.explanation: A brilliant callback payoff — the Bible Michael threw at her becomes the 'miracle' that restored her sight. The story is (1) a callback to Michael's failed exposure attempt, (2) a reference to the episode's religious/Commandments theme, and (3) characterizes Maggie as someone who will maintain a scam to its most ridiculous conclusion. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.4+.

jokes.text: You know, we're saying good-bye, and this is actually the first time that I feel you can really see me. / Yeah. / We're just two people staring at each other fully exposed in a court of law.
jokes.setup: Michael and Maggie say goodbye in the courthouse after she's been removed from the case
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'I feel you can really see me' — meaning emotional vulnerability — is literally true (she can now see him since her temporary blindness has cleared), and they ARE literally exposed in a court of law. The double meaning operates perfectly.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully layered closing scene for the Michael/Maggie storyline. 'You can really see me' — literally (her sight has returned) and emotionally. 'Fully exposed in a court of law' — literal (they're in the courthouse) and metaphorical (their ethical violations are exposed). The line works on every level. One of the episode's stronger literary jokes. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: On the next Arrested Development: Barry gets a sign from God. / I will obey your will. I will lead a good life. / Well, the hell with this then!
jokes.setup: 'On the next' teaser — Barry gets a sign from God and resolves to be good
jokes.punchline: The instant he makes his resolution, he sees the Ten Commandments (now apparently somewhere he can see them) — the literal list of rules — and immediately abandons his resolution: 'Well, the hell with this then!'
jokes.explanation: A strong 'On the next' gag — the format itself is a running AD bit. Barry's immediate reversal upon seeing actual moral standards is perfect character comedy, and the Ten Commandments callback brings the episode's central prop back for a final laugh. 'Well, the hell with this then!' is very quotable.

jokes.text: Maggie is replaced with a new, less generous, prosecutor. / I was made to understand there were grilled cheese sandwiches here.
jokes.setup: The 'on the next' teaser introduces a new, tougher prosecutor to replace Maggie
jokes.punchline: His first line is asking about grilled cheese sandwiches — a callback to Buster's interrogation where the cheese was the leverage — implying either Buster has been talking about this, or the prosecutor is himself food-motivated
jokes.explanation: An exceptional closing joke. The callback to the grilled cheese is unexpected and perfectly timed — a 'less generous' prosecutor whose first concern is the cheese. It rewards the viewer who tracked the Buster subplot. The economy is 9/10 — one line, huge callback payoff. Strong closing gag.

tmdb_overview: Gob attempts to make good on his promise to make the family yacht disappear. Meanwhile, Lindsay and Maeby try to track down Lindsay's grandmother, Tobias seeks George Sr.'s respect, and Michael attempts to fire Kitty, who claims to have information that could bring down the company.
insight: Wordplay and character collision drive 48 jokes across 22 minutes—1.93 per minute, elite density.
